## Overview
This document records and justifies the key behavioral assumptions applied in the IRRBB (Interest Rate Risk in the Banking Book) EVE/NII measurement framework. Behavioral assumptions are critical for accurately capturing the optionality embedded in customer products (non-maturity deposits, fixed-rate loans with prepayment options, etc.) under standard interest rate shock scenarios.

---

## 1. Core Deposit Assumptions (Non-Maturity Deposits – NMDs)

### 1.1 Core vs. Volatile Split
| Deposit Type | Core % | Volatile % | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Transaction / Checking Accounts | 85% | 15% | Historical runoff analysis over 10-year window; low interest elasticity observed during rising rate periods. |
| Savings Accounts | 75% | 25% | Moderate sensitivity; some behavioral decay observed during prolonged low-rate environments. |
| Money Market Deposit Accounts (MMDA) | 60% | 40% | Higher sensitivity due to competitive rate-chasing behavior; peer-bank pricing analysis supports lower core assumption. |
| Time Deposits (CDs) | 100% | 0% | Contractually fixed; no core/volatile split required for term products. |

### 1.2 Core Deposit Decay / Runoff
- **Method**: Exponential decay model calibrated to historical volume data.
- **Average Life Assumption (for core portion)**:
  - Transaction: 7 years
  - Savings: 5 years
  - MMDA: 3 years
- **Justification**: Based on internal regression analysis (2015–2025) showing transactional deposits exhibit the slowest decay, MMDA the fastest. Aligned to EBA/BCBS standard industry benchmarks.

### 1.3 Rate-Setting Behavior (Pass-Through)
See Section 2 below for details on NMD repricing and pass-through assumptions.

---

## 2. Pass-Through Rate Assumptions

### 2.1 Asset Pass-Through (Loans)
| Product | Up-Rate Pass-Through | Down-Rate Pass-Through | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Prime-Based Loans (Variable) | 100% | 100% | Contractually tied to Prime/WAC; immediate pass-through. |
| Adjustable-Rate Mortgages (ARM) | 100% | 100% | Fully indexed (SOFR/COFI); periodic caps modeled separately. |
| Fixed-Rate Loans (New Origination) | 85% | 70% | New origination rates respond slower to market moves due to competitive lag; asymmetric for downward shocks. |
| Fixed-Rate Loans (Existing Book) | 0% | 0% | No contractual repricing; cash flows fixed until maturity. |
| Credit Card Receivables | 95% | 80% | High pass-through due to floating-rate nature, but some floor effects in down-rate scenarios. |

### 2.2 Liability Pass-Through (Deposits)
| Product | Up-Rate Pass-Through | Down-Rate Pass-Through | Lag (months) | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Transaction Accounts | 40% | 30% | 3 | Low rate sensitivity; historical beta of ~0.4 in rising rate environments; slower adjustment downward. |
| Savings Accounts | 50% | 40% | 2 | Moderate beta (0.5); competitive pressures drive up-rate moves, but limited downward adjustment. |
| MMDA | 70% | 60% | 1 | High competitive sensitivity; close to wholesale rate behavior. |
| Time Deposits (Brokered) | 95% | 95% | 0 | Near-full pass-through for brokered/market-rate deposits. |
| Retail Time Deposits (Renewal) | 60% | 50% | 1 | Customer inertia and relationship-based pricing lower pass-through. |

### 2.3 Justification Methodology
Pass-through betas are derived from multivariate regression of internal product rates versus benchmark rates (Fed Funds / SOFR / swap curve) over the past 10 years. Asymmetry is captured by separating up-rate vs. down-rate regimes. Results are validated against peer group surveys and regulatory benchmarking studies.

---

## 3. Prepayment Assumptions

### 3.1 Residential Mortgages – CPR (Conditional Prepayment Rate)
| Shock Scenario | Base CPR | Up 200bp CPR | Down 200bp CPR |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fixed-Rate Mortgages (30yr) | 6.0% | 4.0% | 12.0% |
| Fixed-Rate Mortgages (15yr) | 8.0% | 5.5% | 14.0% |
| ARM Products | 10.0% | 12.0% | 8.0% |

**Justification**:
- In a declining-rate environment (-200bp), refinancing incentives increase sharply, driving higher CPR for fixed-rate products.
- In a rising-rate environment (+200bp), refinancing disincentivizes, reducing CPR.
- ARMs have higher base CPR due to periodic reset features; behavior reverses under shock scenarios.
- Model calibrated using S-curve / logit regression on historical prepayment data (last 8 years), with inclusion of HPI (Home Price Index) and refinancing incentive ratio (WAC vs. prevailing market rate).

### 3.2 Consumer Loan Prepayments
| Product | Base CPR | Up 200bp CPR | Down 200bp CPR | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Auto Loans (New) | 8.0% | 7.0% | 12.0% | Lower rate sensitivity than mortgages; vehicle turnover drives base CPR. |
| Auto Loans (Used) | 12.0% | 10.0% | 16.0% | Higher turnover; more sensitive to rate differences. |
| Personal Loans (Unsecured) | 10.0% | 8.0% | 15.0% | Rate-driven prepayment, but constrained by credit availability. |
| Student Loans (Private) | 5.0% | 4.0% | 8.0% | Lower prepayment sensitivity; regulatory/forbearance effects. |

### 3.3 Commercial / Business Loans
| Product | Base CPR | Up 200bp CPR | Down 200bp CPR | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| CRE Mortgages | 4.0% | 3.0% | 6.0% | Prepayment penalties / lockout periods reduce sensitivity. |
| C&I Lines (Fixed) | 6.0% | 5.0% | 9.0% | Moderate; relationship banking reduces elasticity. |

## 4. Severity/Frequency Analysis

### 4.1 Key Findings
- **High Frequency, Low Severity**: External Fraud (28 events, avg $55K) and Execution/Process Mgmt (28 events, avg $60.7K) dominate by count but have moderate per-event costs.
- **Low Frequency, High Severity**: Internal Fraud (12 events, avg $320K) has the highest average severity, driven by a single large event of $2.1M.

## 2. Assurance Workstreams

### Workstream A: Governance & Framework (Weeks 1-3)

| Activity | Scope | Evidence Required | Regulatory Reference |
|----------|-------|-------------------|---------------------|
| A.1 Board & Committee Oversight | Review ICAAP ownership, committee minutes, challenge documentation | Board meeting minutes, ICAAP policy approval records | SS31/15 §2.1, CRD Art. 76 |
| A.2 Risk Appetite Framework | Assess risk appetite statement, limits, and breach escalation | Risk appetite statement, limit framework, breach logs | SS13/16 §3.1, SREP GL §2.2 |
| A.3 Three Lines of Defence | Review roles, responsibilities, and independence | RACI matrix, job descriptions, organisational charts | SS31/15 §3.1, CRD Art. 76(5) |
| A.4 Risk Culture Assessment | Evaluate risk culture indicators and tone from the top | Culture survey results, conduct risk data | SREP GL §2.2, EBA GL on internal governance |

### Workstream B: Risk Identification & Measurement (Weeks 2-5)

| Activity | Scope | Evidence Required | Regulatory Reference |
|----------|-------|-------------------|---------------------|
| B.1 Risk Taxonomy | Review completeness of risk inventory against business model | Risk register, business model mapping | SS31/15 §4.1, SREP GL §3.1 |
| B.2 Materiality Assessment | Validate materiality thresholds and methodology | Materiality policy, threshold documentation | SS31/15 §4.4, SREP GL §3.2 |
| B.3 Credit Risk Coverage | Assess credit risk quantification, IRB validation, IFRS 9 overlays | PD/LGD models, ECL calculations, credit risk reports | SS13/16 §4, CRR Art. 107-134 |
| B.4 Market Risk Coverage | Review market risk methodologies, FRTB alignment | VaR models, NPV sensitivity, trading book | SS13/16 §5, CRR Art. 325-377 |
| B.5 Operational Risk Coverage | Assess op risk quantification, scenario analysis, RCSA | RCSA results, loss data, scenario analysis | SS13/16 §6, CRR Art. 310-324 |
| B.6 IRRBB Coverage | Review interest rate risk in banking book measurement | EVE/NII sensitivity, ALM reports | SS13/16 §7, CRR Art. 84-86 |
| B.7 Concentration Risk | Assess concentration risk identification and limits | Concentration reports, large exposure monitoring | SS13/16 §8, CRR Art. 81-85 |
| B.8 Liquidity Risk | Review liquidity risk framework, LCR/NSFR, ILAAP alignment | Liquidity reports, ILAAP document, contingency funding plan | SS13/16 §9, CRR Art. 411-428 |

### Workstream C: Capital Adequacy & Stress Testing (Weeks 3-6)

| Activity | Scope | Evidence Required | Regulatory Reference |
|----------|-------|-------------------|---------------------|
| C.1 Pillar 1 Calculation | Verify Pillar 1 RWAs and capital ratios | COREP returns, RWA calculations | CRR Art. 92, CRR Part 3 |
| C.2 Pillar 2a Assessment | Review PRA buffer methodology and calibration | PRA buffer letter, ICAAP capital assessment | SS31/15 §5.1, CRD Art. 104 |
| C.3 Internal Capital Target | Assess target capital levels and buffer adequacy | ICAAP capital target calculation, Board minutes | SS31/15 §5.9, Basel Pillar 2 Principle 5 |
| C.4 Stress Testing Framework | Review scenario design, methodology, assumptions | Stress testing policy, scenario documentation | SS31/15 §6.1, SREP GL §5.1 |
| C.5 Adverse Scenario Analysis | Assess adverse scenario impact on capital | Adverse scenario results, sensitivity analysis | SS31/15 §6.4, CRD Art. 73 |
| C.6 Reverse Stress Testing | Review reverse stress testing approach and results | Reverse stress test results, breach point analysis | SS31/15 §6.8, SREP GL §5.2 |
| C.7 Capital Forecasting | Verify 3-5 year capital projections and assumptions | Capital plan, financial projections, dividend policy | SS31/15 §7.1, SREP GL §7.1 |
| C.8 Management Actions | Assess management action triggers, feasibility, and timing | Management action plan, contingency measures | SS31/15 §7.5, CRD Art. 128-133 |

### Workstream D: Documentation & Reporting (Weeks 5-7)

| Activity | Scope | Evidence Required | Regulatory Reference |
|----------|-------|-------------------|---------------------|
| D.1 ICAAP Document Review | Assess completeness, clarity, and regulatory alignment | ICAAP document, version history, sign-off | SS31/15 §8.1, SREP GL §8.1 |
| D.2 Traceability Assessment | Verify traceability from risk identification to capital allocation | Traceability matrix, methodology documentation | SS31/15 §8.3, CRD Art. 73 |
| D.3 MI Reporting Review | Assess management information timeliness and accuracy | MI dashboards, board packs, risk reports | SS31/15 §8.6, SREP GL §8.2 |
| D.4 Regulatory Reporting | Review COREP/FINREP/Pillar 3 alignment | Regulatory returns, Pillar 3 disclosure | SS31/15 §8.9, CRR Art. 99-101 |

### Workstream E: Validation & Assurance (Weeks 6-8)

| Activity | Scope | Evidence Required | Regulatory Reference |
|----------|-------|-------------------|---------------------|
| E.1 Independent Validation | Review validation function scope, methodology, findings | Validation reports, model validation documentation | SS31/15 §9.1, CRD Art. 76(6) |
| E.2 Internal Audit Review | Assess IA coverage of ICAAP and risk models | IA audit plan, ICAAP audit reports, issue tracking | SS31/15 §9.6, CRD Art. 74(1) |
| E.3 External Assurance | Engage external auditors for ICAAP review | External audit reports, management letters | SS31/15 §9.9, EBA GL on ICAAP |
| E.4 Supervisory Engagement | Plan regulatory liaison and SREP preparation | Meeting agendas, pre-brief packs, correspondence | SS31/15 §10.1, SREP GL §10.1 |

## 2. Identified Gaps and Mitigation Actions

| Gap ID | Area | Gap Description | Regulatory Impact | Severity | Remediation | Timeline |
|--------|------|----------------|-------------------|----------|-------------|----------|
| GAP-01 | Climate Risk | Limited integration of climate risk into ICAAP scenarios | ECB Guide on climate risk; PRA SS3/19 | Medium | Develop climate stress scenarios and sensitivity analysis | 6 months |

| GAP-03 | Operational Resilience | ICAAP not fully aligned with operational resilience requirements | PRA SS1/21; DORA (EU) | High | Map operational resilience scenarios to ICAAP capital impacts | 6 months |
| GAP-04 | Recovery Planning Integration | Limited linkage between ICAAP stress testing and recovery plan triggers | BRRD Art. 5-9; PRA SS4/19 | Medium | Align ICAAP stress test triggers with recovery plan activation points | 4 months |
| GAP-05 | ESG Risk Quantification | Insufficient quantification of ESG transition and physical risks | ECB Guide on climate risk; PRA SS3/19 | Medium | Develop ESG risk quantification models | 12 months |
| GAP-06 | ICAAP Challenge Process | Limited documentation of board challenge to ICAAP assumptions | SS31/15 §2.3; SREP GL §2.1 | High | Strengthen board meeting documentation and challenge records | 3 months |
| GAP-07 | Model Validation Backlog | Delays in independent validation of capital models | CRD Art. 76(6); SS31/15 §9.1 | High | Prioritise validation of high-impact capital models | 6 months |
